title: Thyme cough syrup
subtitle: A simple kitchen syrup for dry, tickly coughs
excerpt: Fresh thyme simmered in water and stirred into honey. A kitchen syrup long made for dry, tickly coughs and a sore chest. The thyme softens the cough; the honey coats the throat. About 40 minutes' work, mostly waiting for the simmer.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), thyme entry, antiseptic, expectorant, antispasmodic action on the bronchial tubes. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), thyme for coughs and chest complaints. German Commission E monograph on Thymi herba, confirms traditional use for upper respiratory catarrh and bronchitis.

content.text: Thyme syrup is a sweet, dark syrup of fresh thyme simmered in water and stirred into honey. A kitchen tradition long made for dry, tickly coughs and a sore chest. The thyme softens the cough; the honey coats the throat and keeps the syrup good in the fridge for about three weeks. About 40 minutes' work, mostly waiting for the thyme to simmer.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy: culinary amounts of thyme in cooking are safe. Therapeutic-dose thyme, repeated large cups of thyme tea or daily thyme syrup throughout pregnancy, is traditionally classified as an emmenagogue herb at high doses, and is best discussed with a midwife or herbalist before use.

Children under two: do not give honey-based preparations to children under two years. Honey can carry Clostridium botulinum spores.

For school-age children, half a teaspoon of the syrup (2.5 ml) up to three times daily is within the traditional paediatric range. Always consult a GP or paediatric herbalist for a child's persistent cough.

See your doctor for: a cough lasting more than three weeks, coughing up blood or discoloured phlegm, breathlessness at rest, fever above 38°C for more than three days, or any cough in a child under five that gets worse rather than better.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Thyme
items.prepNote: fresh thyme, a large handful of sprigs

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: raw honey if available; add once the liquid is below 60°C

items.name: Lemon juice
items.prepNote: optional, adds vitamin C and balances the sweetness

content.text: Put {{thyme}} of fresh thyme sprigs in the small saucepan. Pour {{water}} of water over the thyme and bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Put a lid on the pan and simmer gently for 15 to 20 minutes, the water will reduce and turn a dark olive-green colour and smell strongly of thyme.

Strain the liquid through a fine sieve into a jug, pressing the thyme sprigs gently with a spoon to extract all the liquid. Discard the spent herb. Let the liquid cool until it is just warm to the touch, around 50 to 60°C. Do not add honey to hot liquid; temperatures above 60°C destroy the enzymes and some of the beneficial compounds in raw honey.
Stir {{honey}} of honey into the warm thyme liquid until completely dissolved. Add the lemon juice if using. Pour into a sterilised glass jar with a tight lid. Label with the date.

Adults: 1 teaspoon (5 ml) up to four times daily, taken neat and swallowed slowly, or dissolved in a small amount of warm water if preferred. The traditional course is five to seven days for an acute cough. Do not use continuously beyond two weeks without reassessing whether the cough needs medical attention.

Keeps refrigerated in a sealed jar for 3 weeks. Discard if it smells fermented or the colour changes noticeably. Do not store at room temperature, the honey alone is not concentrated enough to preserve an aqueous preparation indefinitely.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for a cough lasting more than three weeks, any cough producing blood or green phlegm, breathlessness at rest or on minimal exertion, or fever that persists beyond three days. Skip this preparation for infants and toddlers under two (honey). Discuss with a midwife before using during pregnancy at therapeutic doses.
